<!-- headroom:learn:start -->
## Headroom Learned Patterns
*Auto-generated by `headroom learn` on 2026-06-03 — do not edit manually*

### Implementation Preferences
*~120,000 tokens/session saved*
- When adding new tools to the benchmark suite, mirror the existing `codex` and `claude` implementation patterns exactly; do not propose or implement new benchmark infrastructure.
- For `headroom init`, global flags must be placed before the target (e.g., `headroom init -g opencode`) to avoid CLI parsing errors.

### Environment
*~45,000 tokens/session saved*
- Use `python3` or `.venv/bin/python`; the `python` command is not available in this environment.
- venv creation requires `python3 -m venv --without-pip .venv` followed by manual `get-pip.py` installation as `ensurepip` is unavailable.
- Run tests via `.venv/bin/python -m pytest` to ensure the local `headroom` package is correctly resolved in `sys.path`.

### File Structure & Facts
*~25,000 tokens/session saved*
- `ToolTarget` enum values are defined in `headroom/install/models.py`, and agent path helpers are in `headroom/install/paths.py`.
- OpenCode configuration is stored at `~/.config/opencode/opencode.jsonc` (note the `.jsonc` extension).
- Large files (>40KB) including `headroom/cli/init.py` and `headroom/compress.py` should be read using surgical line ranges to save tokens.

### Tooling Guidance
*~15,000 tokens/session saved*
- If `maturin develop` fails with obscure errors, run `rm -rf target/` before retrying.
- Windsurf integration relies on `.windsurf/rules/headroom.md` and Skills rather than the standard JSON hook system used by other agents.

### Testing Context
*~5,000 tokens/session saved*
- GPT-5.5 and 5.3 are the preferred external models for testing tool-calling robustness across different agent implementations in this project.

<!-- headroom:learn:end -->


<!-- headroom:rtk-instructions -->
# RTK (Rust Token Killer) - Token-Optimized Commands

When running shell commands, **always prefix with `rtk`**. This reduces context
usage by 60-90% with zero behavior change. If rtk has no filter for a command,
it passes through unchanged — so it is always safe to use.

## Key Commands
```bash
# Git (59-80% savings)
rtk git status          rtk git diff            rtk git log

# Files & Search (60-75% savings)
rtk ls <path>           rtk read <file>         rtk grep <pattern>
rtk find <pattern>      rtk diff <file>

# Test (90-99% savings) — shows failures only
rtk pytest tests/       rtk cargo test          rtk test <cmd>

# Build & Lint (80-90% savings) — shows errors only
rtk tsc                 rtk lint                rtk cargo build
rtk prettier --check    rtk mypy                rtk ruff check

# Analysis (70-90% savings)
rtk err <cmd>           rtk log <file>          rtk json <file>
rtk summary <cmd>       rtk deps                rtk env

# GitHub (26-87% savings)
rtk gh pr view <n>      rtk gh run list         rtk gh issue list

# Infrastructure (85% savings)
rtk docker ps           rtk kubectl get         rtk docker logs <c>

# Package managers (70-90% savings)
rtk pip list            rtk pnpm install        rtk npm run <script>
```

## Rules
- In command chains, prefix each segment: `rtk git add . && rtk git commit -m "msg"`
- For debugging, use raw command without rtk prefix
- `rtk proxy <cmd>` runs command without filtering but tracks usage
<!-- /headroom:rtk-instructions -->
